---
title: "tidyMacro"
subtitle: "High-Performance Vector Autoregression in R"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Overview}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
---



`tidyMacro` is an R package for fast estimation of Structural Vector Autoregression (SVAR) models. The core is written in C++ via RcppArmadillo, exposing parallelized bootstraps through OpenMP for maximum performance.

```r
devtools::install_github("muhsinciftci/tidyMacro")
```

## Key Features

- **Zero extra dependencies** — all econometrics implemented from scratch in C++
- **Publication-ready plots** — every output is a `ggplot2` object, fully customizable after the fact
- **Parallel bootstraps** — OpenMP-backed; uses $n-1$ cores by default (`n_threads = 1` to disable)
- **Tidy-first workflow** — import and clean data with `tidyverse`, pass to C++ via `as.matrix()`

## Identification Methods

### Recursive (Cholesky) Ordering

The most common identification approach. The lower-triangular Cholesky factor of the residual
covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is used as the structural impact matrix:

$$B_0^{-1} = P \quad \text{where} \quad \Sigma = PP'$$

Variables ordered first are assumed to respond contemporaneously to those ordered later.
Confidence bands are computed via **residual** or **wild bootstraps**, with an optional
**bias-corrected** variant following Kilian (1998).

**Relevant functions:** `fVAR()`, `fwoldIRF()`, `fcholeskyIRF()`, `fbootstrapChol()`,
`fbootstrapCholCorrected()`, `fplotirf_chol()`, `fevd_chol()`, `fhistdec()`

### Long-Run Restrictions (Blanchard–Quah)

Identification is achieved by imposing that certain shocks have **zero long-run effect** on
selected variables. The long-run impact matrix is

$$C(1) = (I - A_1 - \cdots - A_p)^{-1} B_0^{-1}$$

and its lower-triangular Cholesky factorization pins down the structural matrix.

**Relevant functions:** `fbqIRF()`, `fbootstrapBQ()`, `fplotirf_bq()`

### External Instruments (Proxy-SVAR)

An external instrument $z_t$ — correlated with the target structural shock but orthogonal to
all others — is used to construct the first-stage projection following
Mertens & Ravn (2013) and Stock & Watson (2012):

$$\hat{\varepsilon}_{1t} = \Pi z_t + \text{residual}$$

The identified shock is then scaled to give unit impact. Confidence bands use
**moving-block bootstraps (MBB)**.

**Relevant functions:** `fVAR()`, `fbqIRF()`, `fbootstrapBQ()`, `fplotirf_bq()`

---

## Replications

The package ships with four published datasets and step-by-step replications.

| Study | Identification | Key Result |
|---|---|---|
| [Bloom (2009)](Replications/Bloom2009.html) | Recursive ordering | Uncertainty shocks cause sharp, short-lived recessions |
| [Galí (1999)](Replications/Gali1999.html) | Long-run restrictions | Technology shocks reduce hours in the short run |
| [Beaudry & Portier (2014)](Replications/BeaudryPortier2014.html) | Long-run restrictions | News shocks drive comovement in investment and consumption |
| [Kaenzig (2021)](Replications/Kaenzig2021.html) | External instrument | OPEC announcements shift oil prices and propagate globally |

---

## Full Tutorial

The [main vignette](Vignette.html) walks through every step: data import, VAR estimation,
lag selection, impulse responses, variance decomposition, and historical decomposition.
